content.text: The public cloud is the deployment model most people picture when they say "the cloud": computing resources — servers, storage, databases, networking, and hundreds of higher-level services — owned and operated by a third-party provider and made available to any organization over the internet on a pay-per-use basis. AWS is the world's largest public cloud, running infrastructure across 35+ geographic regions and serving millions of customers simultaneously from the same physical hardware — while keeping each customer's data and workloads completely isolated from every other.
What Is the Public Cloud?

In the public cloud model, the provider owns and manages all physical infrastructure — the data centers, servers, cooling systems, power feeds, and network cables. Customers access that infrastructure through APIs, consoles, and SDKs, and they pay only for what they consume. There is no upfront capital expenditure for hardware, no need to forecast capacity years in advance, and no responsibility for physical security or hardware lifecycle management.
What makes the model work economically is
multi-tenancy

: the same physical servers host workloads from thousands of different customers simultaneously. This pooling allows the provider to amortize the cost of expensive hardware and data centers across a massive customer base, passing the savings on as low per-unit pricing that no individual organization could achieve by building its own data center. From the customer's perspective, however, it feels like dedicated infrastructure — because logical isolation enforced at the hypervisor, network, and identity layer means no customer can see or affect another's data or compute.
AWS Public Cloud Architecture

attrs.alt: AWS Public Cloud — Multi-Tenant Global Infrastructure diagram showing customer accounts, AWS regions with availability zones, global backbone, and edge services
attrs.title: AWS Public Cloud — Multi-Tenant Global Infrastructure

content.text: Each AWS account forms an isolated tenancy boundary. Multiple accounts share the same physical hardware inside a Region's Availability Zones, but VPC network isolation and IAM identity controls ensure that no traffic or data leaks between tenants. The AWS Global Backbone — a private fiber network — connects all Regions and Edge locations without customer traffic ever touching the public internet between AWS facilities.
How Multi-Tenancy Works
Multi-tenancy in AWS operates at three distinct layers that together provide security equivalent to — and often stronger than — dedicated physical hardware:
Hypervisor isolation (Nitro System).

AWS runs a custom bare-metal hypervisor called the Nitro System, implemented in dedicated hardware chips rather than software running on the host CPU. Nitro enforces memory and CPU isolation between virtual machines with no shared code path between tenants. Even a host-level vulnerability cannot propagate across the Nitro boundary.
Network isolation (Amazon VPC).

Every customer workload runs inside a Virtual Private Cloud with its own private IP address space. VPC routing tables, security groups, and network ACLs ensure that no two VPCs can communicate unless you explicitly configure a peering connection or transit gateway — even if they share the same physical switch.
Identity isolation (AWS IAM).

Every API call carries a signed identity token scoped to an AWS account. IAM policies are evaluated before any resource is accessed. There is no way to call an API in another account without that account's owner explicitly granting cross-account access via a resource-based policy or IAM role trust relationship.
Storage isolation.
When an EBS volume or S3 object is deleted, AWS cryptographically zeroes the storage blocks before reallocating them. Former data cannot be recovered by the next tenant assigned those blocks.
AWS Global Infrastructure

AWS's physical footprint is the backbone that makes the public cloud model viable at scale. Understanding the three-tier hierarchy — Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge locations — is essential for designing resilient, low-latency architectures.
Tier
Count (2025)
Purpose
Latency between nodes
Region
Independent geographic deployments; data sovereignty boundary; contains 2+ AZs
~60–200 ms between Regions (cross-continent)
Availability Zone (AZ)
One or more discrete data centers with independent power, cooling, and networking; HA building block
< 2 ms between AZs within a Region
Edge / PoP
CloudFront CDN, Route 53 Anycast DNS, WAF/Shield, Global Accelerator termination points
< 10 ms to end users in most metropolitan areas

Regions are independent failure domains: a natural disaster, power outage, or infrastructure failure in us-east-1 has no impact on eu-west-1. Availability Zones within a Region are connected by private high-speed fiber with sub-2ms latency, making synchronous multi-AZ replication practical for databases, file systems, and load balancers.
Logical Isolation Mechanisms in Practice
Creating an Isolated Network with Amazon VPC

Every AWS account starts with a default VPC in each Region. For production workloads you create a custom VPC with a CIDR block you control, then subnet it across Availability Zones. The following AWS CLI commands create a VPC, add a public subnet, attach an internet gateway, and route public traffic:

# 1. Create the VPC
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc \
  --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=vpc,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=prod-vpc}]' \
  --query 'Vpc.VpcId' --output text)

# 2. Create a public subnet in us-east-1a
SUBNET_ID=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --cidr-block 10.0.1.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

# 3. Attach an internet gateway so instances can reach the internet
IGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-internet-gateway --query 'InternetGateway.InternetGatewayId' --output text)
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" --internet-gateway-id "$IGW_ID"

# 4. Route all outbound traffic from the subnet to the internet gateway
RTB_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-route-tables \
  --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=$VPC_ID" \
  --query 'RouteTables[0].RouteTableId' --output text)
aws ec2 create-route \
  --route-table-id "$RTB_ID" \
  --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --gateway-id "$IGW_ID"

echo "VPC: $VPC_ID  Subnet: $SUBNET_ID  IGW: $IGW_ID"
IAM: Least-Privilege Identity Boundary

Every AWS API call is authenticated and authorized by IAM. By default, a new IAM user or role has zero permissions — you must explicitly grant access. This least-privilege default means that even if an attacker obtains credentials, they can only act within the permissions those credentials carry. The following policy allows an application role to read from a specific S3 bucket and nothing else:
The Nitro Hypervisor

AWS's Nitro System offloads hypervisor functions (network I/O, storage I/O, security) to dedicated hardware chips on the physical host. Because the isolation logic runs in silicon rather than software, it is not subject to software vulnerabilities and does not consume any host CPU cycles — meaning customers get nearly all of the physical server's compute capacity. Nitro also enables bare-metal instance types (e.g.,
i4i.metal
) that give customers direct, non-virtualized access to the hardware while still maintaining isolation at the Nitro card level.
Key Characteristics of the Public Cloud
On-demand self-service.
Provision compute, storage, or a managed database in seconds through the console, CLI, or SDK — no human approval needed from the provider.
Broad network access.
All services are reachable via standard protocols over the internet, or privately over AWS Direct Connect dedicated circuits from your data center.
Resource pooling.
Physical resources are shared across many customers with no customer awareness of which specific hardware they are on. You can request a Region or AZ preference, but not a specific server.
Rapid elasticity.
Capacity scales up or down in minutes — or automatically via Auto Scaling — to match actual demand. You are not constrained by hardware you purchased three years ago.
Measured service.

Usage is metered and billed at fine granularity — per-second for compute, per-GB for storage, per-million requests for APIs. You pay only for what you use, with no minimum commitment unless you opt in to Reserved Instances or Savings Plans for a discount.
Benefits of the Public Cloud
No upfront capital cost.

Renting compute converts a CapEx data-center investment into a predictable OpEx line item. A startup can launch on AWS for tens of dollars per month rather than hundreds of thousands for physical servers.
Global reach in minutes.
Deploying to a new continent takes hours, not the months required to negotiate a data-center contract, ship hardware, and rack-and-stack servers.
Economies of scale.

AWS purchases hardware in quantities that no individual organization can match, continuously passing the resulting cost reductions to customers. AWS has cut EC2 prices over 100 times since launching in 2006.
Managed undifferentiated heavy lifting.

Services like RDS, ElastiCache, and OpenSearch handle patching, replication, backups, and failover automatically — work that would require a dedicated ops team to perform manually on self-managed infrastructure.
Built-in high availability.

Multi-AZ deployments distribute workloads across independent failure domains. Most managed services (S3, DynamoDB, Route 53) replicate data across three or more AZs automatically, providing 99.99%+ durability without any additional configuration.
Continuous innovation.

AWS releases hundreds of new features and services per year. Organizations running on AWS gain immediate access to capabilities — AI/ML, quantum computing, satellite ground stations — that would be impossible to build or procure independently.
Inherited compliance.

AWS holds SOC 2 Type II, PCI DSS Level 1, HIPAA, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, and 140+ other compliance certifications. Customers inherit the compliance coverage of the physical and hypervisor layers, reducing their own audit scope significantly.
Trade-offs and Considerations
Perceived security concerns.

Some regulated industries or executives are initially uncomfortable placing sensitive data on shared hardware. This concern is generally misplaced — the Nitro isolation model has a strong security track record — but it does require documented evidence of the provider's controls to satisfy auditors.
Data residency constraints.

Some regulations (GDPR, data localization laws in certain countries) require that personal data remain within a specific geographic boundary. AWS Regions provide the boundary, but you must configure your architecture to respect it — data does not stay in a Region unless you design it to.
Variable latency.

Traffic between your on-premises systems and AWS travels over the public internet unless you provision Direct Connect. Internet latency is non-deterministic, which can be a problem for real-time trading systems, industrial control systems, or applications requiring sub-millisecond round trips.
Vendor dependency.

Deep use of AWS-proprietary services (DynamoDB, SQS, Step Functions) creates coupling to the AWS API surface. Migrating to another provider — or even back on-premises — requires significant re-architecture.
Cost unpredictability without governance.

Pay-per-use is a benefit, but it also means that a misconfigured workload, forgotten NAT gateway, or unexpected traffic spike can generate a large bill. Organizations need cost governance tooling (AWS Cost Explorer, Budgets, tagging policies) to prevent bill shock.
Connectivity dependency.

Applications running entirely in the public cloud require reliable internet or Direct Connect access. Locations with poor connectivity — remote manufacturing plants, ships at sea, mining sites — may not be suitable without edge computing supplements like AWS Outposts or Local Zones.
Getting Started: Launching Your First EC2 Instance
The following AWS CLI commands launch an Amazon Linux 2023 EC2 instance in your default VPC, open port 22 for SSH access, and confirm the instance is running. Replace
YOUR_KEY_PAIR
with a key pair you have already created in the target region:

# Retrieve the latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI ID in us-east-1
AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners amazon \
  --filters \
    'Name=name,Values=al2023-ami-2023*-x86_64' \
    'Name=state,Values=available' \
  --query 'Images | sort_by(@, &CreationDate) | [-1].ImageId' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1)

# Create a security group that allows SSH from your IP only
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name my-first-sg \
  --description "Allow SSH" \
  --query 'GroupId' --output text)

MY_IP=$(curl -s https://checkip.amazonaws.com)/32
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id "$SG_ID" \
  --protocol tcp --port 22 --cidr "$MY_IP"

# Launch a t3.micro instance (free-tier eligible)
INSTANCE_ID=$(aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id "$AMI_ID" \
  --instance-type t3.micro \
  --key-name YOUR_KEY_PAIR \
  --security-group-ids "$SG_ID" \
  --query 'Instances[0].InstanceId' \
  --output text)

echo "Launched: $INSTANCE_ID"
Wait for the instance to enter the running state, then retrieve its public IP:

aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids "$INSTANCE_ID"

PUBLIC_IP=$(aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids "$INSTANCE_ID" \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].PublicIpAddress' \
  --output text)

echo "SSH: ssh -i ~/.ssh/YOUR_KEY_PAIR.pem ec2-user@$PUBLIC_IP"

# Terminate when done to stop billing
# aws ec2 terminate-instances --instance-ids "$INSTANCE_ID"
Shared Responsibility in the Public Cloud

AWS uses the Shared Responsibility Model to divide security obligations between the provider and the customer. In the public cloud, AWS handles the security of the cloud infrastructure itself; customers are responsible for what they put into it and how they configure it.
AWS Manages (Security OF the Cloud)
You Manage (Security IN the Cloud)
Physical data center security (guards, cameras, biometrics)
Guest OS patching on EC2 instances you manage
Hardware lifecycle and failure replacement
IAM users, roles, and policies (who can do what)
Nitro hypervisor isolation between tenants
VPC security groups, NACLs, and routing rules
Global network and power infrastructure
Encryption of data at rest and in transit
Managed service availability (RDS, S3, Lambda)
Application code security and data classification
Compliance and Certifications

AWS maintains a large portfolio of third-party audit certifications covering its physical infrastructure and core services. When you build on AWS, your audit scope shrinks because the physical and hypervisor layers are already certified. Key certifications relevant to most organizations include:
SOC 2 Type II.
Annual audit of security, availability, and confidentiality controls across AWS infrastructure and services. Reports are available under NDA through AWS Artifact.
PCI DSS Level 1.

The highest level of the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard. Relevant if you process credit card transactions — AWS provides a Responsibility Summary document showing which PCI controls AWS handles versus the customer.
HIPAA eligibility.

AWS signs Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) for HIPAA-covered workloads. A set of HIPAA-eligible services is documented; using only those services satisfies the infrastructure portion of HIPAA Technical Safeguards.

International information security management standards. ISO 27017 covers cloud-specific controls; ISO 27018 specifically addresses protection of personally identifiable information in public cloud environments.
FedRAMP.
US federal government security authorization. AWS GovCloud (US) and commercial Regions hold FedRAMP High authorizations for a subset of services, enabling federal agencies to operate on AWS.
Cost Model: From CapEx to OpEx

Traditional data center investment follows a capital expenditure (CapEx) model: buy servers, depreciate them over 3–5 years, and operate them at whatever utilization rate you can achieve. The public cloud converts this to operational expenditure (OpEx) — a metered utility bill. The practical implications:
Pay-per-second compute.
EC2 instance billing starts when the instance launches and stops when it terminates. A batch job that runs for 43 seconds costs 43 seconds of compute, not a full minute or hour.
Spot Instances for deep discounts.
AWS sells unused capacity at up to 90% off On-Demand prices via Spot Instances. Batch processing, CI/CD pipelines, and stateless web tiers are ideal Spot candidates.
Reserved Instances and Savings Plans for predictable workloads.
Committing to 1- or 3-year terms saves 30–70% versus On-Demand. Savings Plans offer flexibility to change instance family, OS, and Region while maintaining the discount.
Cost visibility tooling.

AWS Cost Explorer, Cost and Usage Reports (CUR), and AWS Budgets provide per-service, per-tag, and per-account cost breakdowns. AWS Organizations enables chargeback across multiple accounts in an organization.
When to Choose the Public Cloud
The public cloud is the right choice for the vast majority of workloads, but the fit is strongest when these conditions hold:
You need to launch or scale quickly without waiting for hardware procurement.
Demand is unpredictable or highly variable — e.g., e-commerce with seasonal traffic spikes, SaaS with rapid user growth.
You want access to managed AI/ML, analytics, or IoT services without building the underlying infrastructure yourself.
You need global reach for end users in multiple continents.
Your organization lacks the staff or budget to manage physical hardware reliably (patching, hardware failures, power redundancy, fire suppression).
Common Misconceptions
"The public cloud is less secure than on-premises."

Most data breaches in cloud environments result from misconfiguration (open S3 buckets, overly permissive IAM policies) — not from provider-level failures. AWS's physical security, Nitro isolation, and patching discipline typically exceed what most organizations can achieve in their own data centers.
"Public cloud is always more expensive than owning hardware."

Total cost of ownership (TCO) analysis must include hardware, power, cooling, physical space, staff for maintenance and operations, and the opportunity cost of capital tied up in equipment. For most workloads below a very large scale, cloud TCO is lower. The calculus can shift for very stable, large-scale workloads — which is why companies like Dropbox and Twitter have moved some workloads back to owned infrastructure.
"All public cloud data is accessible to the provider."

AWS employees do not have access to customer data stored in S3, RDS, or EBS. Data is encrypted at rest and in transit by default or via customer-managed keys (AWS KMS). For the highest assurance, AWS Nitro Enclaves provide a provably isolated compute environment with no persistent storage and no outbound network, suitable for processing sensitive credentials or private keys.
Multi-AZ High Availability Pattern

The standard public cloud availability pattern for a web application distributes every stateful and stateless layer across at least two Availability Zones. A failure in one AZ — caused by a power outage, cooling failure, or network partition — triggers automatic failover within seconds rather than requiring manual intervention.

# Multi-AZ RDS MySQL — automatic failover to standby in ~60s on AZ failure
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-mysql \
  --db-instance-class db.t3.medium \
  --engine mysql --engine-version 8.0 \
  --master-username admin \
  --master-user-password "$(openssl rand -base64 24)" \
  --allocated-storage 100 --storage-type gp3 \
  --multi-az \
  --db-subnet-group-name prod-subnet-group \
  --vpc-security-group-ids sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --backup-retention-period 7 \
  --deletion-protection

# Application Load Balancer spanning two AZs
aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name prod-alb \
  --subnets subnet-aza subnet-azb \
  --security-groups sg-alb \
  --scheme internet-facing \
  --type application

# Auto Scaling Group across both AZs — min 2 ensures one instance per AZ
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name prod-asg \
  --launch-template LaunchTemplateId=lt-abc123,Version='$Latest' \
  --min-size 2 --max-size 10 --desired-capacity 2 \
  --availability-zones us-east-1a us-east-1b \
  --target-group-arns arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:111122223333:targetgroup/prod/abc123

With this pattern, losing an entire AZ results in the ALB routing all traffic to the surviving AZ, the RDS standby being promoted automatically, and Auto Scaling launching replacement instances — all within 60–120 seconds, well within the recovery time objective of most production systems.
AWS Organizations and Multi-Account Strategy

Production organizations use AWS Organizations to manage multiple accounts under a single billing and policy hierarchy. Separating environments into distinct accounts limits blast radius — a misconfiguration in development cannot touch production because the two accounts are independent isolation boundaries.
Management account.
Root of the organization, used only for billing, consolidated cost reporting, and attaching Service Control Policies (SCPs). No workloads run here.
Log archive account.
CloudTrail, Config, and VPC Flow Logs from every account delivered here. Developers in workload accounts cannot delete or tamper with audit logs.
Security tooling account.
AWS Security Hub, GuardDuty, and AWS Config aggregator run here with delegated administrator access, providing a centralized security posture view across the organization.
Workload accounts (one per environment per service).
Each application gets separate dev, staging, and production accounts. Quota exhaustion or misconfiguration in one account cannot affect others.

Service Control Policies (SCPs) attached at the Organizational Unit (OU) level enforce guardrails that no IAM policy in a child account can override. An SCP can prevent any account from disabling CloudTrail logging or creating resources outside approved Regions — even if the account's root user tries.
Observability: Metrics, Logs, and Traces
Visibility into public cloud infrastructure relies on three pillars. AWS provides native tooling for each:
CloudWatch Metrics.

Every AWS service publishes operational metrics at 1-minute granularity. EC2 CPU utilization, RDS FreeStorageSpace, ALB RequestCount, and Lambda Errors are all available automatically. Alarms page on-call when thresholds are breached and trigger Auto Scaling when load increases.
CloudWatch Logs Insights.

Application logs, VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail API events, and managed service logs stream to CloudWatch Log Groups. Logs Insights provides an SQL-like query language to aggregate and filter gigabytes of logs in seconds without managing separate log infrastructure.
AWS X-Ray (distributed tracing).

Adds trace context propagation across Lambda, ECS, EC2, and API Gateway to produce service maps and latency breakdowns. When a request is slow, X-Ray identifies which downstream service (DynamoDB call, external HTTP, cold start) is responsible.
AWS CloudTrail.

Records every API call made to AWS services, including the caller's identity, source IP, timestamp, and request parameters. CloudTrail is the control-plane audit log — it answers "who changed this security group rule and when?" even months after the fact.
Key Takeaways

Public cloud shares physical infrastructure across many customers; logical isolation is enforced by the Nitro hypervisor (compute), Amazon VPC (network), and IAM (identity) — not by dedicated hardware.
AWS's global infrastructure spans 35+ Regions, 108+ Availability Zones, and 600+ Edge locations connected by a private fiber backbone — all available to any customer via API.
The pay-per-use model converts infrastructure from a capital expense to an operational one, eliminating upfront hardware investment and enabling cost to track demand rather than peak forecast.
AWS carries 140+ compliance certifications covering its physical and hypervisor layers; customers inherit that coverage and reduce their own audit scope.

Choosing public cloud is appropriate for most workloads; exceptions are edge cases with extreme latency requirements, offline operation needs, or regulatory mandates that prohibit shared physical infrastructure — addressed by Private Cloud or Hybrid Cloud deployment models.
